title: Hydration Calculator for Workouts | Hacé Cuentas
h1: How Much Water Should You Drink During Exercise?
description: How much water to drink during exercise? Free hydration calculator based on your weight, intensity, and weather. Includes electrolyte recommendations.
seoKeywords: how much water to drink during workout
intro: Athletic training relies on physiological principles: adaptation, supercompensation, and specificity. Training zones, weekly volume, and recovery form the foundation of performance. Calculate how many milliliters of water to drink per hour during exercise based on duration, intensity, and climate conditions. Includes personalized electrolyte recommendations. Our data is current as of 2026 and reviewed regularly to keep you informed.
keyTakeaway: **General rule**: 400–800 ml/hour during exercise. High intensity and heat: up to 1,200 ml/hour. Under 60 minutes: water only. Over 60 minutes: add electrolytes (500 mg sodium/L).
useCases: You're preparing your water bottle for a run and need to know how much to bring.
You train in summer and dehydrate quickly in the heat.
You need a hydration plan for an endurance race or long-distance event.
You want to know whether sports drinks or plain water will work best.
You're a coach planning hydration strategy for your team on hot days.

faq.q: How much water should I drink during exercise?
faq.a: The general guideline from ACSM is **400–800 ml/hour** depending on your body weight, exercise intensity, and climate. Adjust based on these factors rather than following a one-size-fits-all approach.

faq.q: Do I need electrolytes during workouts?
faq.a: If your exercise lasts **longer than 60 minutes** or you're exercising in **hot weather**, yes. Aim for **500–700 mg of sodium per liter**. For shorter, moderate-intensity workouts in mild conditions, plain water is sufficient.

faq.q: How much water should I drink before exercise?
faq.a: Drink **500 ml about 2 hours before** your workout, then another **200–300 ml about 15 minutes before** starting. This ensures you're well-hydrated and avoids stomach discomfort.

faq.q: Is cold water better than room temperature for hydration?
faq.a: Cold water (5–15°C) absorbs slightly faster and helps lower your core body temperature, making it preferable in hot climates. Room-temperature water works fine in mild conditions.

faq.q: What are the signs of dehydration during exercise?
faq.a: Watch for **dry mouth, intense thirst, dark urine, headache, and excessive fatigue**. If you lose more than 2% of your body weight during exercise, you're dehydrated and should adjust your fluid intake.

faq.q: Should I drink sports drinks or plain water?
faq.a: For workouts **over 60 minutes**, sports drinks provide carbohydrates and electrolytes that enhance endurance performance. For shorter sessions, plain water is adequate.

faq.q: How much water should I drink after exercise?
faq.a: Drink **1.5 liters for every kilogram of body weight lost** during exercise. Weigh yourself before and after your workout to calculate the difference.

faq.q: How often should I drink during exercise?
faq.a: Drink in small amounts frequently—every 15–20 minutes—rather than large amounts at once. This prevents stomach discomfort and maintains better hydration throughout your workout.
Yes, all our calculators are completely free with no registration required.

explanation:
**Athletic Performance and Hydration**

Effective training combines science (physiology, biomechanics) and practical application. Training zones, progressive overload, rest days, and proper nutrition form the complete system.

## How This Calculator Works

This tool calculates your personalized hydration needs during exercise based on three key factors:

- **Body weight**: Heavier athletes lose more fluid through sweat
- **Exercise duration**: Longer workouts require greater fluid replacement
- **Intensity & climate**: High-intensity exercise and hot conditions increase sweat rate significantly

The calculator applies evidence-based formulas from sports science research and alerts you if your values fall outside typical ranges.

## Why Hydration Matters

Proper hydration optimizes athletic performance, regulates body temperature, maintains adequate blood volume, and supports cognitive function. Even mild dehydration (2% body weight loss) impairs endurance capacity and increases injury risk.

## Final Notes

This calculation is a general reference only. For medical advice, clinical decisions, or nutritional guidance specific to your needs, consult a sports medicine physician or registered sports dietitian. Data is current as of 2026 and reviewed periodically.
